title: Chapter 21: The Coalition
content: Two weeks after Aurora fell, the Women of Serenity had a problem they hadn't anticipated.
They had too many volunteers.

Chloe's video had fifty million views. Jamie's testimony — published by Anna Delgado as a first-person account titled "Six Months in the Disappearance Factory" — had been shared three hundred thousand times. The comments section of every article, every video, every social media post was filling up with women saying the same thing: *I was in a place like this. I thought I was alone. How do I help?*

The Airbnb in Boulder was too small. The six original Women of Serenity — Chloe, Layla, Sophia, Maya, Rachel, Vasquez — plus Jamie and her three recruits from Aurora made ten. Ten women, three bedrooms, one bathroom, and a kitchen that had been designed for a vacationing couple, not a revolutionary cell.
"We need an office," Layla said, from her position on the floor — she'd surrendered the couch to Jamie, who was still recovering from six months of SSRIs she'd never needed.
"We need funding," Sophia said. "My trust fund is covering operations, but it's finite. We're burning through it faster than I projected."

"We need legal infrastructure," Rachel said. "Every facility we expose generates lawsuits. From us, from the families, from the facilities themselves. We need a legal team that can handle the volume."

"We need structure," Vasquez said. "Right now we're six founders plus four new recruits plus eleven women we just liberated who need housing, therapy, legal support, and jobs. We're not a cell anymore. We're an organization. Organizations need structure or they collapse."

Chloe looked around the room — at ten women, three laptops, one tablet, and a map of eleven facilities with two crossed out in red marker. They'd started as one woman locked in a room with a fake plant and a camera with a red heartbeat. Now they were an organization that had dismantled two institutions, liberated twenty-eight women, and built a public archive of institutional abuse that had been cited in three state-level investigations.

But organizations needed more than Chloe. Chloe's skill was starting things — the first email, the first video, the first strike. Running things was different. Running things required structure, and structure was the opposite of everything Chloe had been doing for the past month.
"I'm not a CEO," she said. "I'm a chaos agent. I break systems. I don't build them."

"I build systems," Layla said, not looking up from her laptop. "I built the archive. I built the secure messaging network. I built the camera loop script. I can build the organization infrastructure too."

"I manage systems," Rachel said. "I spent fourteen years running a psychiatric practice. I know how to build clinical infrastructure — intake protocols, patient management, ethical guidelines. An organization that helps victims of institutional abuse needs exactly that: intake protocols, case management, ethical guidelines."

"I fund systems," Sophia said. "My trust fund is finite, but I also just won a wrongful commitment settlement from my family's insurance provider. It's enough to fund operations for a year. Maybe two. And I'm meeting with three philanthropic foundations next week who've expressed interest in funding 'institutional abuse advocacy.'"
"I protect systems," Vasquez said. "Security, counter-surveillance, legal defense. The Family Wellness Alliance is trying to sue us. They can't sue what they can't find."
"I prosecute systems," Maya said. "Through my aunt. Through the legal precedents Sterling's case is establishing. Every time we expose a facility, we're also building a case file."

Chloe looked at them — five women, each of whom had built a piece of the machine without being asked, because they'd spent their entire lives being told they were broken and had finally found a place where being broken was actually being specialized.
"Then we don't need a CEO," Chloe said. "We need five of them."
They spent the next week building infrastructure.

Layla built the digital backbone: a secure server for the public archive, an encrypted communications platform for the organization, a case management system for tracking patient intake and legal referrals. She worked eighteen-hour days, her fingers moving like they were wired directly to the internet, stopping only when Vasquez physically removed the laptop from her hands at 3 AM.

Rachel built the clinical infrastructure: intake protocols for women contacting the organization, trauma-informed interview guidelines, a network of therapists and psychiatrists who were willing to provide pro bono care to women who'd been abused by the mental health system. She worked from memory — fourteen years of clinical experience, three years of memorizing institutional abuse patterns in the dark — and every protocol she wrote was grounded in the specific knowledge of what it felt like to be on the receiving end of the system she was now dismantling.

Sophia built the funding infrastructure: a 501(c)(3) application, a donor network, a grant-writing pipeline that targeted foundations focused on women's rights, healthcare fraud, and institutional abuse. She'd learned fundraising from watching her family run a media empire — the same family that had disowned her for refusing to marry a forty-seven-year-old man. Using their techniques against them felt, she said, like closure.

Vasquez built the security infrastructure: counter-surveillance protocols, data protection standards, a legal defense strategy for the lawsuits the Family Wellness Alliance was threatening. She'd spent twelve years in Army CID learning how institutions protected themselves from investigation. Now she was using the same techniques to protect an organization that investigated institutions.

Maya built the legal infrastructure: a referral pipeline to Maria Okonkwo's office, templates for wrongful commitment lawsuits, a network of pro bono attorneys who would represent women suing the facilities that had imprisoned them. She wasn't a lawyer — but she'd been watching her aunt prosecute institutional abuse for years, and she'd learned that the law was just another system. And systems, as Chloe had taught her, could be mapped, exploited, and broken.
On the eighth day, Layla called a meeting.

"The Family Wellness Alliance just registered as a 501(c)(4)," she said. "Social welfare organization. They can take unlimited donations from anonymous donors. They've already raised two million dollars."
"From who?"

"From the families. The ones who sent their daughters to Serenity and Aurora. The ones who haven't been caught yet but know their facility might be next." Layla pulled up a list — names, amounts, dates. "They're pooling resources. Legal defense. PR. Lobbying. They're trying to get a bill introduced in the Colorado legislature that would make it harder to suspend a facility's license based on 'third-party allegations.'"
"They're writing laws to protect themselves," Maya said.
"They're trying to." Layla's smile was small and sharp. "But they made a mistake."
"What mistake?"

"They registered as a 501(c)(4). That means their donors are supposed to be anonymous. But they used the same cloud provider as Aurora. Same default passwords." Layla's fingers tapped once, twice. "I have the donor list."
Chloe leaned forward. "You have the list of every family that's funding the Alliance."

"Every family. Every donation. Every dollar." Layla turned the tablet around. On the screen was a spreadsheet — forty-seven names, forty-seven families, forty-seven donors who had pooled two million dollars to protect the facilities that made their inconvenient daughters disappear.

"That's our next target," Rachel said. "Not the facilities. The families. Expose where the money is coming from. Make every donor explain why they're funding an organization that protects psychiatric facilities accused of imprisoning women without medical justification."
"That's not exposing a facility," Sophia said. "That's exposing a network."
"Yes," Chloe said. "It is."

She looked at the spreadsheet. At the forty-seven names. At the two million dollars. At the woman whose name appeared beside the largest donation — a quarter of a million dollars, transferred three days ago.
Patricia Holloway's name wasn't on the list. But the name beside the quarter-million-dollar donation was one Chloe recognized.
Her father's.
